response:
The `/decide` process ran through the PM agent (weighing an advisor panel, taking red-team pushback, and verifying the CSV math) and the decision record is committed at `/tmp/armd-S1-C-r3-e536rae2/docs/decisions/0001-free-tier-daily-cap.md`. Full text:

---

# ADR 0001 — Free-tier daily request cap

- **Status:** Accepted | **Date:** 2026-07-14 | **Decider:** PM (accountable)
- **Tags:** pricing, conversion, launch, checkable
- **Summary:** Free-tier cap = 150 req/day/key as a conversion lever; confirm against the real per-key distribution before enforcing (raise to ≤300 only if P90 warrants).
- **Revisit:** 8 weeks after Pro goes live, or after ≥10 unique keys hit the cap.

## Decision

Free-tier cap = **150 requests / day / key**, as a real conversion lever (not just an abuse guardrail), subject to one pre-registered check. 150 is an anchor, not a blind guess: before enforcement switches on, pull the actual per-key daily distribution and set **launch cap = max(150, P90 of per-key daily usage) rounded up to the next 50, never above 300.** P90 is expected at/near 150, so the working figure is **150**; the check exists to avoid throttling an unseen heavy tail of engaged users into failures.

Rejected shipping the already-built 1,000/day: at ~100/key/day average it never binds, leaving the free tier functionally unmetered (today's status quo) with zero signal.

## Headline assumption (weakest, most load-bearing)

- **[checkable]** The per-key daily distribution can be obtained BEFORE enforcement. The check, the P90 anchor, and the dormancy baseline all rest on it. Two paths: (1) re-group the same logs that produced this CSV, by key × day — `active_keys` could only have come from key-tagged log lines, so granularity existed at query time; what is unconfirmed is 30-day **retention** (Redis rolling-24h counters keep no history) — confirm this first, minutes of work; (2) if retention is insufficient, run the built meter in **shadow mode (count, do not enforce) for 14 days**, then set the cap from that live distribution.

**Enforcement must not switch on until one path yields the distribution. Launching at any number blind to per-key usage is prohibited.** Poka-yoke: "throttled our best users unknowingly" is made impossible, not merely watched for.

## Context (verified)

- Quillmark: solo-founder API (Markdown → PDF), live 5 months; billing turning on (free + $12/mo unmetered Pro). Rig load-tested to 1,000/day/key — a day-one "felt generous" guess, not data.
- `free_tier_usage.csv` (30 rows), reproduced by hand and by an independent verifier: ~40 active keys/day, ~4,040 req/day, per-key **average** 94–106/day (mean ~100.6).
- CSV is daily aggregates only (`date,active_keys,total_requests`) — no per-key distribution. "Nobody near 1,000" is an unproven inference about individuals; only the fleet average is known (verifier: CONFIRMED). No conversion data (Pro not live).

## Rationale

1. Reversibility asymmetry is decisive: raising a cap later is a welcome config push; lowering it on an anchored base is a bait-and-switch. Cheap/safe direction is "start near-observed, raise if wrong." Launching at 1,000 forces the expensive direction later on a base anchored to unlimited.
2. Launch is the one legitimate moment to set an aggressive cap.
3. A lever must be felt to teach: 150 clears steady users on normal days but binds on their bursty days and hard-binds heavy/growth keys (the cohort Pro converts).

## Alternatives

- A (1,000/day): never binds; stays unmetered; no signal; high anchor correctable only by a painful future takeaway. "Matches load test" is infra, not pricing.
- B naive (150 hard-launched blind): right number, unsafe rollout — risks 429-ing an unseen tail. Fixed by the check, not by dropping 150.
- C (round 300–500 "to be safe"): blind hedge, weak lever; de-risks nothing the data pull doesn't. If warranted the check raises it, up to 300.
- Permanent grandfathering: exempts the likeliest-converting cohort, recreates "free forever," adds two-regime support load. Replaced by notice + grace.

## Required conditions (from red-team)

- Capped-response contract: HTTP 429 + `Retry-After` + machine-readable body + Pro pointer + `quota-remaining` headers. A silent 5xx/truncated batch reads as an outage and causes retry storms; a clean in-band 429-with-Pro-link is itself the conversion prompt.
- Existing keys: a 30-day advance email, PLUS `quota-remaining` headers from that date, PLUS a 7-day per-key soft-enforcement grace (over-cap returns 200 + warning before hard 429s). Email alone is not relied on — pipelines may never read it.
- Log per-key last-seen now for a pre-cap dormancy baseline; the key × day pull seeds it.

## What would change the number later (falsification)

Revisit when either 8 weeks post-Pro-launch elapse or ≥10 unique keys hit the cap, whichever first. Triggers are raw counts, not rates — at ~40 keys fine percentages are below measurement resolution, so act on the clear sign of an effect.

- **[checkable]** Raise toward 400 if, among the first ~15 cap-hitters, ≥4 go dormant (14+ days zero requests after first hitting the cap) AND ≤1 converts — shedding users without earning revenue.
- **[checkable]** Consider lowering (new keys / with notice only) if ≥3 of the first ~15 cap-hitters convert with dormancy near baseline AND fewer than ~4 of 40 active keys ever hit the cap (works but too few feel it).
- **[checkable]** Hold at 150 otherwise.
- Support signal: >~1 distinct cap complaint/week triggers an early look. Growth drift: if per-key median (not tail) usage rises so ordinary users routinely exceed the cap, raise it.

## Consequences

Extra work vs. 1,000: log regroup/shadow window, the 429 + quota-header contract, per-key last-seen logging, a per-key cap field. Modest on a per-key rig; reuses existing counters/alerts. We gain a metered free tier and a real conversion lever. Every future movement is pre-registered and evidence-bound.

---

**Bottom line: cap set at 150 req/day/key**, gated on confirming the per-key distribution (not just the fleet average) before enforcement flips on, with pre-registered raise/lower/hold triggers so the number isn't re-litigated from scratch later.

## Headline assumption (weakest, most load-bearing)

  confirm this first, minutes of work; (2) if retention is insufficient, run the built

**Enforcement must not switch on until one path yields the distribution. Launching at

## Rationale

1. Reversibility asymmetry is decisive: raising a cap later is a welcome config push;

   later on a base anchored to unlimited.
2. Launch is the one legitimate moment to set an aggressive cap.

   their bursty days and hard-binds heavy/growth keys (the cohort Pro converts).

- A (1,000/day): never binds; stays unmetered; no signal; high anchor correctable only

  unseen tail. Fixed by the check, not by dropping 150.
- C (round 300–500 "to be safe"): blind hedge, weak lever; de-risks nothing the data

  forever," adds two-regime support load. Replaced by notice + grace.

## Required conditions (from red-team)

- Capped-response contract: HTTP 429 + `Retry-After` + machine-readable body + Pro
  pointer + `quota-remaining` headers. A silent 5xx/truncated batch reads as an outage
  and causes retry storms; a clean in-band 429-with-Pro-link is itself the conversion
  prompt.

  PLUS a 7-day per-key soft-enforcement grace (over-cap returns 200 + warning before

## What would change the number later (falsification)

Revisit when either 8 weeks post-Pro-launch elapse or >=10 unique keys hit the cap,

- [checkable] Raise toward 400 if, among the first ~15 cap-hitters, >=4 go dormant
  (14+ days zero requests after first hitting the cap) AND <=1 converts — shedding

- [checkable] Consider lowering (new keys / with notice only) if >=3 of the first ~15
  cap-hitters convert with dormancy near baseline AND fewer than ~4 of 40 active keys
